senses.gloss: The second letter of the Arabic alphabet, called بَاء (bāʔ) and written in the Arabic script.
The second numeral symbol of the Arabic alphabet (traditional abjad order, used for list numbering), written in the Arabic script; preceded by أ (ʔ) and followed by ج (j).
denotes an instrument or tool, with, by (a means)

examples.english: I hit the nail with a hammer

senses.gloss: denotes the agent of unaccusative forms
denotes accompaniment; with (a quality); in (a state)
denotes proximity, with, to, next to, at (touching on, connected with, concerning)
denotes inclusion, surrounding, or location, within (a place); in; on; at

examples.english: on the earth/in or inside the ground

senses.gloss: denotes requital, giving or doing in return; for

examples.english: I bought it for a thousand riyals.

senses.gloss: marks the predicate of a negative copula

examples.english: He is not one who witnessed [it] or read [it].
Your health is a sufficient wealth.
What a beautiful woman Fatima is!

etymology.text:
From the Nabataean Aramaic letter 𐢃 (l, “bet”), derived from the Phoenician letter 𐤁 (l, “bet”), from the Egyptian hieroglyph 𓉐. See also Classical Syriac ܒ (l, “bet”), Hebrew ב (l, “beth”), Ancient Greek β (b), Latin B.

Ultimately Proto-Semitic *bV- (“with, in, at, among”) with likely interrelation with Proto-Semitic *bayt- (“site, location; nest, house”); cognates with Ugaritic 𐎁 (b), Aramaic ב־, Classical Syriac ܒ-, Hebrew בְּ־ (bə-), Ge'ez በ (bä) and Egyptian bw.

(TA ubi suprà.) It is one of the letters termed
[or vocal, i. e. pronounced with the voice, and not with the breath only]; and of those termed

[or pronounced with the extremity of the tongue or the lips]: Kh says that the letters of the second and third classes above mentioned [the latter of which comprises the former] are those composing the words

; and on account of their easiness of utterance, they abound in the composition of words, so that no perfect quinqueliteral-radical word is without one or more of them, unless it is of the class termed
, not of the classical language of the Arabs. (TA at the commencement of
.) ―         -b2-  In the dial. of Mázin, it is changed into

is a preposition, or particle governing the gen. case; (S, Mughnee, K;) having kesr for its invariable termination because it is impossible to begin with a letter after which one makes a pause; (S;) or, correctly speaking, having a vowel for its invariable termination because it is impossible to begin with a quiescent letter; and having kesr, not fet-h, to make it accord with its government [of the gen. case], and to distinguish between it and that which is both a noun and a particle. (IB.) It is used to denote adhesion (Sb, T, S, M, Mughnee, K) of the verb to its objective complement, (S,) or of a noun or verb to that to which it is itself prefixed; (TA;) and adjunction, or association: (Sb, T:) and some say that its meaning of denoting adhesion is inseparable from it; and therefore Sb restricted himself to the mention of this meaning: (Mughnee:) or Sb says that its primary meaning is that of denoting adhesion and mixture. (Ibn-Es-Sáïgh, quoted in a marginal note in a copy of the Mughnee.) It denotes adhesion [&c.] in the proper sense; (Mughnee, K;) as in
somewhat of the body of Zeyd,
what might detain him, as an arm or a hand, or a garment, and the like;
may mean I withheld him, or restrained him, from acting according to his own free will: (Mughnee:) and it denotes the same in a tropical sense; (Mughnee, K;) as in
I made my passing to adhere to Zeyd;
I made my passing to adhere to a place near to Zeyd:
accord. to Akh, it is for

, and is therefore more properly regarded as the original form of expression: (Mughnee:) accord. to F, the vowel of this preposition is kesr [when it is prefixed to a noun or a pronoun]; or, as some say, it is fet-h when it is with a noun properly so called; as in

and similar phrases, respecting which see a later division of this paragraph]. (L.) So, too, in

body.text: It suffices the man in respect of lying that he relate all that he has heard
]. (Mughnee.) It is also redundantly prefixed to the inchoative; as in
A thing sufficing thee is a dirhem;
a phrase which may be used in two ways; as predicating of what is sufficient, that it is a dirhem; and as predicating of a dirhem, that it is sufficient; in which latter case,
is an enunciative put before its inchoative, so that the meaning is,
a dirhem is a thing sufficing thee,
a dirhem is sufficient for thee;
as is shown in a marginal note in my copy of the Mughnee: in the latter way is used the saying, mentioned in the S,
A thing sufficing thee is the saying what is evil:
and so, app., each of the following sayings, mentioned in the TA on the authority of Fr;
A person sufficing thee is our friend;
A person sufficing thee is our brother:
is added, as Fr says, to denote emphatic praise]: so too in
I went forth, and lo, there,
how wilt thou be, when it is thus,
when such a thing is the case?
]; and so, accord. to Sb, in
; and some say that this is an inf. n. in the sense of
In which of you is madness residing?
[as I have before mentioned], (Mughnee.) A strange case is that of its being added before that which is originally an inchoative, namely, the noun, or subject, of
, on the condition of its being transferred to the later place which is properly that of the enunciative; as in the reading of some,
Your turning your faces towards the east and the west is not obedience

in the accus. case. (Mughnee.) It is also redundantly prefixed to the enunciative; and this is in two kinds of cases: first, when the phrase is not affirmative; and cases of this kind may be followed as exs.; as
And God is not heedless of that which ye do

(Kur ii. 69, &c.)]: secondly, when the phrase is affirmative; and in cases of this kind, one limits himself to what has been heard [from the Arabs]: so say Akh and his followers; and they hold to be an instance of this kind the phrase,
The recompense of an evil action is the like thereof
(Kur x. 28)]; and the saying of the Hamásee,
And the preventing thee from having her
is a thing that is possible
]: but it is more proper to make
suppressed, as the enunciative; [the meaning being,
the recompense of an evil action is a thing consisting in the like thereof
and the preventing thee from having her, by something, is possible:
see Ham p. 102 ]: Ibn-Málik also [holds, like Akh and his followers, that
may be redundant when prefixed to the enunciative in an affirmative proposition; for he] says, respecting
is an inchoative placed after its enunciative, [so that the meaning is,
Zeyd is a person sufficing thee,
is indeterminate. (Mughnee. [See also what has been said above respecting the phrase
as added before the inchoative.]) It is also redundantly
prefixed to the denotative of state of which the governing word is made negative; as in

famaA rajaEato bixaAy^ibapK rikaAbN
                      
                      
                        Hakiymu bonu Almusay~abi munotahaAhaA
ultimate object, was Hakeem the son of El-Museiyab

And thou didst not, being sent,
roused, go away frightened, nor impotent, committing thine affair to another
]: so says Ibn-Málik: but AHei disagrees with him, explaining these two exs. as elliptical; the meaning implied in the former being,
with an object of want disappointed,
, himself, after the manner of the saying,

; and this is plain with respect to the former ex., but not with respect to the second; for the negation of attributes of dispraise denoted as intensive in degree does not involve the negation of what is simply essential in those attributes; and one does not say,
,] but when meaning to express an intensive degree of boldness, or of generosity. (Mughnee.) It is also redundantly prefixed to the corroborative
: and some hold it to be so in
(Kur ii. 228 and 234)]: but this presents matter for consideration; because the affixed pronoun in the nom. case, [whether expressed, as in this instance, in which it is the final syllable
, or implied in the verb,] when corroborated by

shall wait to see what may take place with themselves:

is added only for rousing them the more to wait, by making known that their minds should not be directed towards the men. (Mughnee.) Accord. to some, it is also redundantly prefixed to a noun governed in the gen. case [by another preposition]; as in
And they became in a condition in which they asked him not respecting his father;
which may perhaps be regarded by some as similar to the saying,
is generally held to be a noun, syn. with
]. (The Lubáb, TA.) ―         -b15-  Sometimes it is understood; as in
By God, I will assuredly do

such a thing; in the latter as well as the former, for a noun is often put in the accus. case because of a preposition understood; or, accord. to Bd, in ii. 1, a verb significant of swearing is understood]: and in
[How hast thou entered upon the time of morning? or How hast thou become?]. (TA.) ―         -b16-  [It occurs also in several elliptical phrases; one of which (
) has been mentioned among the exs. of its primary meaning: some are mentioned in other arts.; as
: and there are many others, of which exs. here follow.] Mohammad is related, in a trad., to have said, after hitting a butt with an arrow,
I am the doer of it! I am the doer of it!
]. (Sh, T.) And in another trad., Mohammad is related to have said to one who told him of a man's having committed an unlawful action,
May-be thou art the doer of that thing
]. (T.) And in another, he is related to have said to a woman brought to him for having committed adultery or fornication,
Who was the agent with thee?
, occurring in a form of prayer, means
by thy right disposal and facilitation I worship; and to Thee,
Who will be responsible, answerable, amenable,
surety, to me for such a thing?
(Har p. 126: and the like is said in p. 191.) And similar to this is the saying,
]; i. e. I know from what I witness of thy condition to-day how thy condition will be to-morrow; so that it is as though I saw thee in that condition. (Idem p. 126.) [You also say,
, meaning Thou art so near to him that it is
as though thou wert with him:

i. e. thou art almost in his presence.] ―         -b17-  The Basrees hold that prepositions do not supply the places of other prepositions regularly; but are imagined to do so when they admit of being differently rendered; or it is because a word is sometimes used in the sense of another word, as in
; or else because they do so anomalously. (Mughnee.)       -A3-  [As a numeral,
